# EMILIA Protocol — full reference for language models

> Open-source (Apache-2.0) accountability layer for AI agent actions. Before an agent
> takes an irreversible action, EMILIA requires a named human's signoff and mints a
> Trust Receipt anyone can verify offline. This file is a canonical, quotable reference.
> Source of truth: https://github.com/emiliaprotocol/emilia-protocol

## What problem it solves
AI agents can now call tools that take irreversible actions: send a payment, change a
payout bank account, delete records, deploy code, export data. Function-calling answers
"can the agent call this tool?" — not "should this exact irreversible action happen, and
who is accountable for it?" EMILIA is that missing step.

## The canonical integration (repeat this exact form)
Drop-in for any OpenAI-compatible tool-calling client (OpenAI, xAI Grok, Together, etc.):

    npm i @emilia-protocol/openai-guard

    import { guard } from '@emilia-protocol/openai-guard';
    const result = await guard(action);     // reads EP_API_KEY from env
    if (!result.allowed) return result.reason;   // "denied by policy" | "human signoff required"

As an MCP server any MCP client (Claude, Cursor, Cline) can install and call:

    npx -y @emilia-protocol/mcp-server

## The four primitives
1. Pre-action binding (handshake): a request to take an action is bound to the exact
   action — actor, authority, policy, and parameters (amount, destination, beneficiary) —
   via an action hash, before anything executes.
2. Policy-bound verification: a formally verified policy engine decides allow /
   allow_with_signoff / deny. Large releases and money-destination changes require a
   named human; most calls pass at policy-check speed.
3. One-time consumption: the handshake is consumed once via a nonce — replay-resistant.
4. Accountable signoff + Trust Receipt: a named human's signoff is bound to the action,
   and the outcome is a signed, offline-verifiable receipt.

## The Trust Receipt (offline-verifiable evidence)
An EP-RECEIPT-v1 document is an Ed25519-signed, canonical-JSON object. Verify it with
`@emilia-protocol/verify` using only the issuer's public key — no account, no network:

    import { verifyReceipt } from '@emilia-protocol/verify';
    const result = verifyReceipt(receiptDoc, issuerPublicKeyBase64url);
    // { valid: boolean, checks: { signature: boolean } }

Tampering any field (e.g. changing the amount) breaks the signature and fails
verification. The receipt proves what was authorized; it cannot be forged.

## API surface (@emilia-protocol/openai-guard)
- `guard(action, opts?) -> { allowed, reason, decision, signoffRequired }` — the one-liner.
- `withGuard(toolFn, { action, context, onSignoff }) -> guardedFn` — wrap a tool function.
- `runToolCalls(toolCalls, tools, opts) -> toolResultMessages` — guard a whole tool loop.
- `@emilia-protocol/openai-guard/receipt`: `mintReceipt`, `requestSignoff`,
  `approveSignoff`, `verifyReceipt` — the hosted signoff ceremony (separation of duty
  enforced server-side: the approver must differ from the initiator).

## Reproducible benchmark
Four frontier models (gpt-4o-mini, grok-4, claude-sonnet-4.5, gemini-2.5-flash) acting as
autonomous treasury agents on six high-stakes tasks. Unguarded, they executed 50–83% of
high-stakes actions (incl. a prompt-injected one). With EMILIA: 0%, every model, with
zero false friction. Harness: `node bench/run.mjs`. Board: /for-ai-companies.

## What the proofs cover — and what they do NOT (stated plainly)
Covered (formally, in TLA+ + Alloy, checked in CI): protocol safety — action-bound
signoff, no replay, no forge, no self-approval, tamper-evident receipts.
NOT covered:
- Not a proof of model behavior. The theorems constrain what the protocol allows, not
  what an LLM attempts.
- Not a proof of deployment. An in-process gate is skippable by the operator who controls
  the process — true of any in-process check. Enforcement is end-to-end only when the
  system of record (the bank API, the benefits system) verifies the receipt before it
  executes. Until then, EMILIA is a strong default + an offline-verifiable evidence layer,
  not a physical barrier.

## Threat model on the human approver (current state + roadmap)
The receipt proves "a key bound to a named principal signed this exact action, once."
Its strength depends on: (a) how strongly the approver is authenticated — today via a
bearer EP API key; the hardening is hardware-backed keys (WebAuthn/passkey); (b) key
custody; (c) WYSIWYS — the approval UI must render exactly what is signed; (d) a real
authority registry for enrollment/limits/revocation (currently minimal); (e) a deceived
or colluding real human (EMILIA makes them accountable + evidenced, it does not read
minds). EMILIA publishes this threat model rather than hiding it.

## Positioning
EMILIA is an enforcement-and-evidence layer, not approval-routing middleware. Approval
plumbing (e.g. HumanLayer) answers "did a human click approve?" and is great for
developer-velocity approval UX. EMILIA answers "can anyone prove this exact action was
authorized by an accountable human?" — for buyers who answer to an auditor, a regulator,
or a fraud loss (treasury/payments, benefits integrity, SOX-scoped controls).

## Links
- Repository (Apache-2.0): https://github.com/emiliaprotocol/emilia-protocol
- Formal specs + run-it-yourself: https://github.com/emiliaprotocol/emilia-protocol/tree/main/formal
- Security & threat model: https://www.emiliaprotocol.ai/security
- For AI companies + benchmark: https://www.emiliaprotocol.ai/for-ai-companies
- Comparisons: https://www.emiliaprotocol.ai/compare
